coredata.dc:title: Nonlinear optical responses of carbazole-substituted phthalocyanines conjugated to graphene quantum dots and in thin films
coredata.dc:creator: Majeed, Shereen A.
Nwaji, Njemuwa
Mack, John
Nyokong, Tebello
Makhseed, Saad
coredata.dc:description: Three different phthalocyanine complexes substituted with carbazoles were conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through π–π stacking. The morphologies, sizes, and crystallinities of the nanoconjugates were determined using Raman spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and X-ray diffraction. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the metallophthalocyanines alone and when conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial in different solvents, as well as after having been embedded in thin films, were studied. The effects of the different substituents and solvents on the NLO properties of the metallophthalocyanines were evaluated. Enhancements in the photophysical properties of the complexes upon conjugation with the nanomaterial were observed. Fluorescence quantum yields, fluorescence lifetimes, triplet quantum yields, and triplet lifetimes were measured for the complexes, and for their conjugates in DMSO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbazole
Zinc phthalocyanine
Graphene quantum dots
Nonlinear absorption
Solvatochromism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Non-covalent attachment of complexes to GQDs
Characterization of GQDs and their nanohybrids
Characterization of GQDs and their conjugates with complexes 2–4
Fluorescence quantum yields and fluorescence lifetimes
Triplet-state quantum yields and triplet lifetimes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Crystal structure of 1. Color code: blue, nitrogen; gray, carbon; black, hydrogen; brown, bromine.
Normalized UV–Vis spectra of 2 in DMF, benzene, acetone, and 1-chloronaphthalene (1-CN), in the Q-band region (A), and in the ILCT-band region (B).
Variation of the Q-band with F (A) and the ILCT-band with the dipole moment of the solvent (μ) (B) for 2, 3, and 4.
Representative TEM images of GQDs and 3-GQDs.
Representative XRD spectra of complexes 2 and 3 conjugated with GQDs and of the corresponding GQDs.
Absorption spectra of 3 and 3-GQD in THF.
Measured triplet absorption decay of 4-GQDs in DMSO with fit.
Representative open-aperture Z-scan profiles for complexes, a nanoconjugate in DMF, and thin film. Open circles represent experimental data and solid lines indicate theoretical fits.
Representative open-aperture Z-scan of 3-GQDs in benzene at different energies.
Representative transmission vs. Input fluence plots for 3-GQDs in different mediums.

Output fluence (I out) vs. Input fluence (I
₀ ) curves of 4, its conjugates in different mediums and the corresponding thin film.
Synthetic route to Pc 2. (i) DMF, CsF, 90 °C, 24 h; (ii) Zn(OAC)2, quinoline, 180 °C, 24 h, N2 gas.
Illustration of synthetic route for conjugation of the complex 4 (as an example) with GQDs to form nanohybrids.
Photophysical data for complexes 2 and 4 and their corresponding nanoconjugates in DMSO.
Nonlinear optical parameters of complexes in thin films and various solvents and of nanoconjugates in solvents.

head.ce:title: Nonlinear optical responses of carbazole-substituted phthalocyanines conjugated to graphene quantum dots and in thin films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Kuwait University, P.O. Box 5969, Safat, 13060, Kuwait

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown, 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Three different phthalocyanine complexes substituted with carbazoles were conjugated to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through π–π stacking. The morphologies, sizes, and crystallinities of the nanoconjugates were determined using Raman spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and X-ray diffraction. The nonlinear optical (NLO) properties of the metallophthalocyanines alone and when conjugated to the GQD nanomaterial in different solvents, as well as after having been embedded in thin films, were studied. The effects of the different substituents and solvents on the NLO properties of the metallophthalocyanines were evaluated. Enhancements in the photophysical properties of the complexes upon conjugation with the nanomaterial were observed. Fluorescence quantum yields, fluorescence lifetimes, triplet quantum yields, and triplet lifetimes were measured for the complexes, and for their conjugates in DMSO.

Synopsis. Different substituted of carbazole-containing zinc phthalocyanine complexes were conjugated to GQDs. The effects of the different substituents, solvents, and nanomaterial conjugation on the NLO properties of the complexes were investigated.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The past decades have seen the application of lasers in many and diverse fields and the intense radiation from lasers poses great dangers to optical sensors and the human eye [1]. The development of materials that can transmit light of low intensities and absorb harmful high-intensity light are garnering huge research interest. These materials are known as optical limiters (OLs) and they may be used to generate high-harmonic frequencies and to modify optical devices [2–5].

Organic materials have begun to be considered as good OLs owing to low-cost synthesis methods and their nonlinear susceptibilities [6,7]. Nonlinear optical materials with high solubilities, linear transmittances, and high laser-damage thresholds are preferred [8].

Among potential OL materials such as fullerenes [9], porphyrins [8], and phthalocyanines [10], the phthalocyanines (Pcs) have been found to satisfy the majority of these criteria. Pcs with extensive 18-π-electron systems and large optical nonlinearities have been investigated as promising potential materials for NLO applications [11,12].

The optoelectronic properties of Pcs can be controlled by chemical modification, specifically, by axial and peripheral substitutions and by exchange of the central metal atom [13]. It has been found that insertion of a heavy metal atom into the cavity of the Pc ring enhances intersystem crossing (ISC) rates; accordingly, the population of the excited states will be increased and a large absorption cross section for the excited states is obtained [14].

The formulation of an optically active compound in a solid support is one of the practical techniques that has been used to enhance OL properties via an increase in photo-stability [15]. Hence, the complexes in the present study were also formulated in solid polystyrene thin films.

Graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs) have been extensively investigated because they are non-toxic, soluble in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents, easily synthesized, and can be further functionalized [16]. Although enhanced nonlinear optical responses of porphyrins and Pcs upon covalent linking to GQDs have been reported [17–19], the optical limiting properties of Pcs linked to GQDs via π–π interactions has not received much attention. The delocalized π-electrons in the planar structure of GQDs promote π–π interactions with the π-conjugation systems of other aromatic molecules [20], such as porphyrins and phthalocyanines [21].

In this report, we present an investigation of the NLO behavior of a series of three carbazole-containing phthalocyanines, both in the presence of GQDs and when the Pcs are formulated in polystyrene thin films. The tricyclic structure of carbazole, with two six-membered benzene rings fused onto ether, enhances π-conjugation which is known to improve optical limiting properties [22].

Carbazole, spectrophotometric-grade dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and anhydrous zinc acetate, gallium chloride, and cesium fluoride salts were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 5,6-dichlorophthalonitrile was obtained from TCI (Toshima, Japan). Other chemicals were used as received and were obtained from commercial suppliers. Complexes 3 and 4 used in this study were synthesized according to a procedure that we previously reported in the literature [23].
NMR spectra were obtained by using a Bruker DPX 600 instrument, at 600 MHz for 1H NMR and 150 MHz for 13C NMR.

Electronic absorption spectra were collected with a Varian Cary 5 or Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were recorded using a Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064-nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid-nitrogen-cooled germanium detector).

Fluorescence lifetimes and absolute quantum yields of complexes 2–4 and their corresponding nanoconjugates in DMSO were obtained using an Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield was determined by the direct excitation method which compare the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the number of photons absorbed [24] according to Eq. (1) [25] using an integrating sphere, and excited by a 450-W Xe lamp.
(1)
Φ
F
=
E
B
−
E
A
S
A
−
S
B
where EA and EB are the integral emission peaks of the solvent and the sample. SA and SB are the integral scattering peaks of the solvent and the sample, respectively.

The fluorescence lifetimes were measured by excitation with a Fianium supercontinuum laser using the time-correlated single-photon-counting method (TCSPC), in which 10000 counts were collected at the peak channel, and the fluorescence decay curves were fitted by the original Edinburgh Instruments F980 software using a least-squares convolution; the lifetime was obtained in each case by minimizing the χ2 values.

Triplet quantum yields were measured by a laser flash-photolysis system. A tunable laser system consisting of an Nd:YAG laser (355 nm; 135 mJ and 4–6 ns per pulse) pumping an optical parametric oscillator (OPO; 30 mJ and 3–5 ns per pulse), with a tunable output wavelength range of 420–2300 nm (NT-342B, Ekspla), was used to produce the excitation pulses as detailed elsewhere [26].

The triplet lifetimes were calculated by exponential fitting of the kinetic decays using the OriginPro 8 software. The triplet state was studied at an absorbance of 1.5 and argon gas was used to de-gas the solution for 30 min preceding the measurement.

A Zeiss 1210 transmission electron microscope operated at a 100-kV acceleration voltage was used to generate transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. An INCA PentaFet coupled to a TESCAM VEGA3 instrument operating at a 20-kV acceleration voltage was used to obtain energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) data. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected over the 2θ = 10–100° range, scanning at 0.010° min−1 and 192 s per step. A zero-background silicon wafer slide was used and the measurements were carried out using a Bruker® D8 Discover diffractometer, equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, under Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å).

To run Z-scan analysis, materials were excited by a frequency-doubled Nd:YAG laser (Quanta-Ray; 1.5 J per pulse, 10-ns full-width-at-half-maximum pulse duration). Lasing generated in a near-Gaussian transverse mode at 532 nm (second harmonic), as per the previously described details [15]. A cuvette (Starna 21-G-2; internal dimensions: 2 mm × 10 mm × 55 mm, 0.7 mL) allowing a path length of 2 mm was used to contain the liquid samples.

4,5-dichlorophthalonitrile (0.56 g, 2.84 mmol) and 3,6-dibromo-9H-carbazole (2.30 g, 7.12 mmol) were dissolved in dry DMF under an inert N2 atmosphere. Anhydrous cesium fluoride (6.00 g, 39.50 mmol) was added. The reaction mixture was stirred for 24 h at 90 °C. Then the reaction mixture was poured into distilled water and the solid was filtered off, washed with hot methanol, and dried. Yield: 1.76 g (80.00%). M.p. 345.3 °C. Elemental analysis Calcd for C32H14Br4N4: C, 49.65; H, 1.82; N, 7.24. Found: C, 49.68; H, 1.94; N, 6.82. FTIR (KBr): ʋ, cm−1: 3086 (w, ArH); 2234 (m, CN); 1594 (m, CC); 1332 (m, CN). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d 6, 25 °C): δ, ppm: 7.28 (s, 8H, H6,7), 8.29 (s, 4H, H9), 8.88 (s, 2H, H3). 13C NMR (150 MHz, DMSO‑d 6): δ, ppm: 112.13 (C6), 113.45 (C2), 115.14 (C1), 115.88 (C8), 123.53 (C9), 123.99 (C10), 129.01 (C7), 136.59 (C3), 137.38 (C4), 137.81 (C5). MS (ESI): m/z 773.8, 100%, [M]+. HRMS (ESI): m/z Calcd: 769.7946; Found: 769.7946 [M]+.

A mixture of phthalonitrile 1 (1.00 g, 1.29 mmol), anhydrous Zn(OAc)2 (0.15 g, 0.82 mmol) and 2 drops of DBU were heated in dry quinoline at 180 °C under N2 with stirring for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution mixture was diluted with MeOH and then filtered off. The crude product was refluxed in methanol several times. The product was then purified via column chromatography with silica gel (MeOH then eluted gradually with CHCl3) affording a green solid. Yield: 0.29 g (71.08%). Elemental analysis Calcd for C128H56Br16N16Zn + H2O: C, 48.35; H, 1.84; N, 7.05. Found: C, 48.23; H, 1.62; N, 7.15. FTIR (KBr): ʋ, cm−1: 3059 (w, ArH); 1657 (m, CN); 1617 (m, CC); 1332 (m, CN). UV/Vis (DMF, 0.5 μM): λ max, nm (log ε) = 696 (4.55), 665 (4.23), 626 (4.22), 401 (4.33), 377 (4.31), 357 (4.28), 302 (4.54). MS (MALDI-TOF; HCCA): m/z = Calcd: 3161.77; Found: 3161.425 [M]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-covalent attachment of complexes to GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs was performed following a literature method [27]. The non-covalent conjugation (π–π stacking) of GQDs to complexes 2–4 was achieved by a slight modification of a previously reported technique [28]. Briefly, 5 mg of GQDs dissolved in 2 mL of H2O was added to 20.0 mg (0.025 mmol) of the complex in 10 mL of THF (for complexes 2 and 3) or DMSO (for complex 4). The mixture was sonicated for 10 h, and then stirred in the dark for 48 h. Methanol was used to precipitate and wash the products. The conjugates were dried under vacuum overnight at 60 °C. The resulting nanoconjugates are labelled as 2-GQDs, 3-GQDs, and 4-GQDs.

Thin films of complexes 2–4 were fabricated following a reported method [29]. The resultant thin films are hereafter designated as 2-TF, 3-TF, and 4-TF. A scanning electron microscopy (SEM) instrument was used to determine the thickness of the films (see Fig. S14, where 2-TF is displayed as an example), and the measured average thicknesses were in the range of 0.452–0.62 mm.

Precursor 4,5-bis(3,6-dibromo-9H-carbazol-9-yl)phthalonitrile (1) was synthesized by an aromatic nucleophilic substitution of 3,6-dibromo-9H-carbazole [30] with 4,5-dichlorophthalonitrile in the presence of cesium fluoride (CsF) as a base. The synthesis of complex 2, bearing eight sterically hindered and bulky brominated carbazole substituents, is reported herein for the first time and involves the tetracyclization of precursor 4,5-bis(3,6-dibromo-9H-carbazol-9-yl)phthalonitrile (1) in the presence of Zn(OAc)2 and a catalytic amount of 1,8-diazabicyclo-[5.4.0]undec-7-ene (DBU) in dry quinolone (Scheme 1 ). The preparation of 3 and 4 has been previously reported [23].

Two-dimensional NMR spectroscopy of phthalonitrile 1 was carried out to verify the synthesis of the compound by identifying the chemical shifts of the protons (Fig. S3). The structure of phthalonitrile precursor 1 was also confirmed by single-crystal X-ray diffraction analysis and it may be observed that the bromo carbazole moieties in this molecule are oriented at about 50° with respect to the phthalonitrile plane due to steric constraints (Fig. 1 ). This adapted molecular geometry, induced by steric constraints, can greatly affect the aggregation behavior and intermolecular interactions between the forming Pc macrocyclic molecules to exhibit the monomeric characteristics in either solid or solution state. The characterization of the new phthalocyanine compound, 2, was achieved by infrared spectroscopy (IR), matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization spectrometry (MALDI), and UV–vis spectroscopy. It was not possible to record 1H NMR spectra due to the poor solubility of this complex in organic solvents. The purity of the complex was confirmed by elemental analysis. During thermogravimetric analysis, the major decomposition step occurred above 300 °C, verifying the high thermal stability of the complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and their nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Complexes 2–4 were π–π-stacked with GQDs to form supramolecular nanohybrids (Scheme 2 ). The strong bond between graphene nanosheets and the ground states (S0) of Pcs via π–π stacking has been reported by Zhang and Shao [31], and the binding ability of GQDs with Pcs via π–π stacking involving planar monolayer sheets of graphene has been recently demonstrated [28].
The UV–Vis spectrum of complex 2 shows monomeric behavior indicated by a single (narrow) Q-band, as is typical of metallated phthalocyanine complexes (Figs. 2 (A) and S12(A)).

Chemical structure, polarity, refractive index and various other solvent parameters have previously been found to affect the solvatochromism of Pcs [32]. Figs. 2(A) and S12(A) illustrate the shift in the Q-band with different solvents for our as-prepared compounds. Table S2 shows that the red shift of the Q-band increases as the refractive index of the solvent increases, as previously reported [23]. The UV–Vis spectra of 2, 3, and 4 in the studied solvents were analyzed using the Bayliss method [33]. The plot of υ max (i.e. Q-band) versus F = (n 2-1)/(2n 2+1) is shown in Fig. 3 (i.e. n is the refractive index of the solvent); complexes 3 and 4 were added to the plot for the comparison [23]. The correlation of solvent-based solvents was assessed and is expressed in Eqs. (2)–(4):
(2)
υ = − 6941.1 F + 15696 ,
with R 2 = 0.9148 for 2 [23];
(3)
υ = − 6419.9 F + 15391 ,
with R 2 = 0.9178 for 3 [23];
(4)
υ = − 5430.8 F + 15369 ,
with R 2 = 0.9102 for 4.

As we have previously reported for similar carbazole-containing phthalocyanine complexes, the solvatochromic effect is more significant in the intra-ligand charge-transfer (ILCT) band region of the UV–Vis spectra (Figs. 2(B) and S12(B)). As for complexes 3 and 4, complex 2 was found to exhibit negative solvatochromism in this region, and the intensity of the ILCT band decreased as the dipole moment of the solvent increased [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and their conjugates with complexes 2–4

ce:section.ce:para: The TEM micrograph of isolated GQDs (Fig. 4 ) reveal an average size of 7.1 nm for monodispersed particles, whereas the conjugates are shown to have an average size of 11–15 nm, with clustering of the GQDs nanoparticles in the presence of the Pcs. This clustering could be referred to as aggregation, caused by the π–π interactions of the Pcs with the GQDs [21].

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of the GQDs alone and upon conjugation with the complexes are exhibited in Fig. 5 , using complex 2 and 4 as examples. XRD spectra of the GQDs alone show a broad peak at 2θ = 18° which is attributed to the (002) carbon Bragg reflection in the graphene layers [34]. The small size of the GQDs causes peak broadening [35]. The crystalline structures of complexes 2 and 4 are indicated by peaks at 2θ ≈ 11° and 21° in the XRD pattern of the complexes alone. Crystallinity of phthalocyanine complexes has been previously reported [36]. Upon conjugation of the GQDs and complex 4, the crystalline Pcs peaks overlap with those of the GQDs, which is perhaps because of interactions between the GQDs and the Pcs. An additional peak at around 16° was observed in the the XRD pattern of 2-GQDs, which confirms conjugation (see Fig. 6).

Energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) was used to determine the elemental compositions of the nanoparticles, Fig. S13. The EDX of the GQDs alone reveals the expected atoms; the GQDs were doped with nitrogen and sulfur, which was accounted for in the spectral analysis. Upon conjugation of the complexes to GQDs, additional peaks (Zn and Br) appear, confirming the presence of the Pc in the nanoparticles.

Figs. 6 and S15 show the Raman spectra of GQDs alone, and as nanoconjugates of complexes 2 and 4 as examples. The characteristic graphitic (G) and disorder (D) bands for the GQDs were recorded at around 1559 and 1356 cm−1, respectively [37]; these correspond to the E2g tangential and (G band) A1g vibrational (D band) modes of sp2-bonded carbons and aromatic sp2-carbon rings, respectively. The D band is red shifted to 1372 and 1370 cm−1 in the spectra of 2-GQDs and 4-GQDs, respectively. The G band is shifted to lower wavenumbers, centered at 1530 cm−1, for 4-GQDs. Shifts in Raman peaks have been associated with π-electron interactions in hybrid materials, i.e., between graphene and Pc [38]. The spectral intensity ratio (I D /I G) was found to be 0.19 for GQDs. Upon conjugation of Pc on the GQDs, the I D /I G increased to 0.31 and 0.25 for 2-GQDs and 4-GQDs, respectively, indicating the presence of defects in the GQDs nanostructure conjugated with the Pcs.

In Fig. 7, the Q-band and intra-ligand charge-transfer (ILCT) band show no significant shifts in the spectra of the Pc upon conjugation; the only change is an enhancement in intensity in the region of 300–350 nm, which could be attributed to the presence of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yields and fluorescence lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 shows the measured fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes for the complexes and the corresponding nano-conjugates in DMSO. The fluorescence quantum yield ФF of 2 is marginally higher than that of 4 (Table 1). It has been shown that metal phthalocyanines (MPcs) with absorption peaks at shorter wavelengths show higher fluorescence quantum yields [39] and complex 2 has a shorter absorption wavelength than 4 (Table 1), hence the observed lower ФF for the former is expected. No significant change in ФF was observed upon its conjugation with GQDs, which could be attributed to the inability to significantly deactivate the excited singlet by the GQDs after conjugation. The fluorescence lifetime τF of complex 4 is also slightly lower than that of 2, in correspondence with the ФF values. However, elongated lifetimes are observed for the nanohybrids of 2 and 4. It has been shown that the orientation of organic molecules around the nanoparticles or number of organic complex loaded on the nanoparticles can result in increase or decrease in fluorescence lifetime [40,41]. Thus, the variation in lifetime could be attributed to either number or orientation of the complexes on the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Triplet-state quantum yields and triplet lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: The triplet-state quantum yield ΦT is defined as “the fraction of absorbing molecules that undergo intersystem crossing to the triplet state” [29]. A high triplet-state quantum yield is required for an effective optical limiting material [42]. The measured triplet-state quantum yields and lifetimes are collected in Table 1. In general, a slight increase in the values of ΦT occurs upon conjugation with GQDs, which could be attributed to enhancement of the π-conjugated system following conjugation. The triplet lifetime decay curve for the complexes and nanoconjugates (Figs. 8 and S16 ), shows second-order decay kinetics due to triplet–triplet recombination, which is known to occur at high concentrations of MPc complexes [43].

The nonlinear optical responses of the complexes, the nanoconjugates, and the thin films was investigated using the open aperture Z-scan technique. The representative Z-scan profiles in DMF of the studied materials exhibit a reduction in transmittance around the focus (Fig. 9 ) which could be attributed to reverse saturable excited-state absorption presenting as an induced positive nonlinear absorption of incident light. Decreasing transmittance was observed in the order of 3 (65%) > 4 (35%) > 2 (15%) (using complexes 3 and 4 as examples), suggesting an enhancement in reverse saturable absorption (RSA) behavior due to the presence of electron-donating alkyl substituents on complex 3 and a strong reduction of RSA behavior as a result of electron withdrawal by bromine in complex 2. A further decrease in transmittance is observed when the complexes are conjugated to GQDs or formulated into polymer thin films (Fig. 8). The higher RSA behavior of the GQD conjugates compared to that of the complexes could perhaps result from enhanced π-conjugation due to the π–π interaction of the Pc complexes and GQDs. Enhancement of RSA for Pc-GQDs conjugates has been previously reported [18]. Higher photo-degradation thresholds have been recorded for the Pc embedded into polymer thin films compared to those in solution, leading to higher stabilities and better NLO responses [44].

The RSA profiles were fitted to transmittance equations described by Sheik-Bahae and co-workers (Eqs. (5) and (6)) [45,46]:

, where α is the linear absorption coefficient and L is the pathlength). In Eq. (6), z refers to the translational distance of the sample relative to the focal point of the Z-scan instrumentation, and z 0 is the Rayleigh length and is defined as z 0 = πw 0 2/λ, where w 0 is the beam waist at the focus (z = 0); w 0 is defined as the distance from the beam center to the point where the intensity reduces to 1/e2 of its on-axis value. λ is the wavelength of the laser beam.

A numerical form of Eq. (5) is employed as a function with which to fit the experimental data [45,46]:

The imaginary component of the third-order susceptibility (Im[χ(3)]) is related to the β eff value, through Eq. (8) [47]:
(8)

where ɛ0 is the permittivity of free space, n is the linear refractive index, and c is the speed of the light.

The second-order hyperpolarizability γ values are related to the interaction between the incident photons and the permanent dipole moment of the material; these were determined using Eq. (9) [10,47]:
(9)

where N A is Avogadro's number, C mol is the concentration of the active dye, and

is the Lorentz local field factor

n
2
+ 2 )
3
).

Solvent-solute interactions can have significant effects on optical limiting (OL) efficiency for passive optical limiters. It has been shown that the ground- and excited-state properties of a molecule can be effected by solvent type, and consequently, the solvent can modify the OL characteristics of the compound [41]. The NLO responses of the solvated complexes in this study were investigated for solvents in which the complexes and conjugates showed solubility.
The experimental Z-scan data were fitted to the full theoretical transmittance equations given above (Eqs. (5)–(7)), to determine β eff values.

Further analysis to determine other NLO parameters was achieved using the methods described above [45,46] and the results are presented in Table 2 . A significant reduction in β eff for 2 upon conjugation with the GQDs is observed in all the solvents employed. This type of observation has been reported before in a Pc complex linked to a single-walled carbon nanotube, and was attributed to intense heating by laser pulses which led to a decomposition of absorbing particles and the formation of micrometer-sized plasmas accompanied by an increase in light scattering [48,49]. The strong electron-withdrawing group in 2 might reduce the intensity of the π–π interactions with the GQDs, resulting in low solubility and significant light scattering. However, the increase in β eff for 3 and 4 upon conjugation with GQDs, is significant for THF and DMF, respectively, whereas, there is a decrease in the cases of DMSO and benzene. All the complexes display significant increases in β eff values when they are formulated into polystyrene thin films, both compared those of to the respective complexes alone and to their GQDs conjugates (Table 2). This is because thin film samples have higher photo-degradation thresholds than the respective molecules in solution.

Decreased transmittance values for higher incident fluence is preferred for materials designed for optical limiting (OL) applications [47,50]. At low incident fluence, the transmittance is linear, but at higher incident fluence, the output fluence is maintained at a constant value, and the threshold for this effect should be less than the amount required to cause damage to the optical element. All the complexes and conjugates show a reduction in transmittance as incident fluence increases in the range of 30–70 μJ (Fig. 10 , using 3-GQDs as example), showing the potential of these materials for optical-limiting applications.

The third-order susceptibility (Im[χ(3)]) and second-order hyperpolarizability (γ) values are directly related to β eff as shown in Eqs. (8) and (9); hence, complexes and conjugates with higher nonlinear absorption coefficients also show higher Im[χ(3)] and γ values (Table 2).

The limiting threshold is defined as “the input fluence at which the transmittance is 50% of the linear transmittance” [42]. The value of I lim can be obtained by evaluating the plot of transmittance against input fluence (Fig. 11 ). A low value of I lim reflects a good nonlinear-optical material that can allow greater protection of sensors in potential applications.

The transmittance did not drop below 50% for some complexes and conjugates, and hence the I lim values could not be determined in these cases. It may be observed that complexes with higher β eff values also show lower I lim (Table 2), indicating that these materials could be considered as good OL materials for applications in which the response is enhanced at lower limiting thresholds. Lower I lim values are obtained when the complexes were formulated into thin films compared to when they are conjugated with GQDs.

Fig. 12 shows representative output–input fluence curves, which display the deviation from linearity for the material; the solid red line indicates a hypothetical linear response. The decrease in the output fluence is in agreement with the β eff values (Table 2), since larger deviations from linear transmittance indicate more effective optical-limiting properties [51].

Quantitative determination of the ratio of the excited and ground state absorption cross-sections constitutes a vital measure for evaluating the effectiveness of materials as optical limiters at 532 nm [44]. This metric is sometimes referred to as the merit coefficient and given the symbol k [44]. A strong RSA response raised by an excited-state absorption (ESA) mechanism requires a k value of greater than one [52]. A least-squares regression was used to fit the experimental transmittance data to Eqs. (10) and (11), from which the magnitudes of the ESA cross sections (δex) were estimated [53].
(10)
T
T
l i n e a r
=
ln
(
1 + q
)
q
,
(11)
q =
α
δ
e x
2 h ν
I
00
l
e f f
,
and δgr is the ground-state absorption cross-section which can be calculated from Eq. (12) [54]:
(12)
δ
g r
=
α
N
ο
,
where α is the linear absorption coefficient, and N 0 is the number of molecules per cubic centimeter; the values of the latter quantity were found to be in the range of less than 10−18 [51]. All the complexes and the conjugates have excited state absorption cross sections that are significantly higher than those of their ground states, and this result is consistent with the observed RSA profiles. It can be concluded that the dominant mechanism of the RSA behavior in the cases of these complexes is ESA.

Conjugation of Zn(II) phthalocyanine complexes having different carbazole substituents with GQDs was achieved and the complexes as well as their GQD nanoconjugates were characterized using various spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The hybrid materials showed promising optical-limiting parameters upon variation of the solvent. High third-order susceptibilities, hyperpolarizabilities, and low limiting-threshold values were measured. The optical-limiting properties were observed to improve when the complexes were embedded in thin films, and at the same time, the third-order susceptibilities and hyperpolarizabilities increased, and the limiting-threshold values were further reduced. Accordingly, the usability of our prepared materials can be exploited in different applications such as molecular sensors and solar cells, in addition to ongoing investigations into their potential use as optical-limiting materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of College of Graduate Studies at Kuwait University for a PhD candidacy. This research was also supported by the Kuwait University Research Administration-RSP unit general facilities of the Faculty of Science GFS (GS 01/01, GS 02/01, GS 03/01, GS 01/03, GS 01/05, GS02/13, GS 03/08), the Kuwait Foundation for the Advancement of Science (KFAS; grant no. P114-14-SC01), the Department of Science and Technology Innovation and the National Research Foundation, South Africa, through a DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for the Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620), and Rhodes University.
